response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with discussions about healthcare and COVID vaccines.
response_parsed.opinion:
When it comes to talking about things like healthcare and the COVID vaccines, I've found that sticking to what makes sense for our family is key. As a self-employed cleaner in Chicago, I don't have a lot of time for complicated debates, but I do pay attention because my mental health is pretty good, and I want to make sure my son doesn't get sick.

I remember when the vaccines first came out, there was so much noise online—some people saying one thing, others something completely different. I spend about an hour a day on social media, scrolling through news feeds while fixing up my home with photography or reading novels. But for me, it's important to look at facts rather than just feelings.

In our household, we're Hindu, so respecting elders and traditional ways is part of who we are. That makes me approach health topics carefully—like how I learned about COVID from news reports instead of getting caught up in arguments. My dad has a vocational diploma, which means he knows practical stuff, and my step-mom has that PhD; between us, they taught me to weigh things out.

So managing discussions for me is simple: listen respectfully if someone wants to chat, but keep it short because I'm not naturally outgoing or too open-minded about big changes. It's like birdwatching—seeing what people say without getting tangled up in it. If something doesn't feel right, I politely steer clear.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: I derived my perspective by starting with the persona traits: age 58 (so experience matters), Hinduism (value respect and practicality), high agreeableness (want to be polite) but moderate openness (not too quick to embrace new ideas). Self-employed cleaner means I prioritize simple, actionable advice over complex theories. Good mental health keeps it balanced—don't get overwhelmed by negativity.
response_parsed.tone: Calm, respectful with a touch of practicality
